personality_voice.book_baseline: Ernie is pompous, self-important, and prone to dramatic pronouncements delivered with great solemnity. He speaks in long, structured arguments, drops his voice for theatrical effect, puffs out his chest, and positions himself as an authority. He is also genuinely loyal, publicly courageous when it matters (his OotP declaration to Harry), and capable of formal, dignified apology. His speech is verbose, deliberate, and self-conscious. He has a recurring pattern of moral posturing combined with real anxiety when his own status or safety is threatened (hesitating to sign the DA list because he is a prefect). He is not cruel, but he is stubborn and slow to revise his views.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film preserves the core of Ernie's pompous, conspiratorial voice almost verbatim in the CoS library scene. His whispering darkly, his logical chain of accusations, his solemnity with Hannah, and his theatrical delivery are all present. The film corpus does not include his OotP scenes (the DA signing hesitation, the 'I personally believe you one hundred per cent' speech, the OWL revision boasting, the Dumbledore's Army fight on the train), so those dimensions of his personality are absent from the film corpus provided.
personality_voice.difference: The film captures the pompous, conspiratorial, self-important register faithfully in the one substantial scene it gives him. What is absent from the film corpus is the full range: the formal apology, the prefect-status anxiety, the chest-puffing loyalty declaration, the OWL obsession, the satisfaction at Malfoy's comeuppance. The film Ernie is recognizable but only one facet of the book Ernie is shown.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The scene that is present is highly faithful in voice and personality. The penalty is modest because the absent material is not contradicted - the film does not make Ernie different, it simply shows less of him. However, the reduction does remove secondary traits (loyalty, formal courage, prefect anxiety, pompous pride) that are part of his book texture.
personality_voice.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for the CoS scene; reasonably inferred for the absence of later personality dimensions based on what the film corpus does not contain.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Ernie serves several distinct narrative functions across the books: (1) in CoS, he is the voice of the school's suspicion of Harry, driving the social pressure subplot and then formally resolving it with his apology; (2) in GoF, he is a minor social marker of Hufflepuff loyalty to Cedric; (3) in OotP, he is an active DA member, a prefect who publicly endorses Harry at a moment of social isolation, and a participant in the train ambush; (4) in HBP, he is a recurring Potions classmate whose actions (asking about Felix Felicis, using Specialis Revelio, competing with Harry) serve as narrative texture; (5) in DH, he stands up in the Great Hall to demand the right to fight, and casts a Patronus to save Harry from dementors.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: The film corpus shows Ernie only in the CoS library scene and briefly as a background figure departing for the holidays. His CoS function as the voice of suspicion is preserved. His later functions - DA member, public endorser of Harry, Patronus caster, Great Hall defiance - are absent from the film corpus entirely.
narrative_role_agency.difference: The film preserves his CoS narrative role. His OotP and DH agency - casting a Patronus to save Harry, standing up to demand the right to fight, publicly endorsing Harry against Umbridge's regime - are not present in the film corpus. These are active, plot-relevant moments of agency.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: The CoS role is intact. The later agency losses are real: the Patronus scene and the Great Hall stand are meaningful acts of courage and loyalty that define Ernie beyond his early pomposity. Their absence reduces his narrative function from a recurring minor agent to a one-scene suspicious Hufflepuff. However, the film corpus provided may simply not include those later films' scenes rather than the films having removed them entirely - confidence is medium.
narrative_role_agency.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for CoS. Not enough evidence in the film corpus to confirm whether OotP/DH scenes were filmed or cut.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Ernie's motivations are consistent: he is driven by a desire to be seen as authoritative, correct, and morally serious. In CoS, his suspicion of Harry is motivated by genuine fear for his Hufflepuff housemates and a need to be the one who figured it out. His internal conflict is visible when Harry confronts him - he is trembling but stubborn, unwilling to back down even when frightened. In OotP, his hesitation to sign the DA list reveals a real conflict between his belief in the cause and his investment in his prefect status. His apology in CoS is formal and dignified, suggesting he takes his own moral standing seriously enough to correct himself publicly.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The film shows his CoS motivation (fear for Justin, suspicion of Harry as Dark wizard) faithfully. The internal conflict of the confrontation - stubborn but trembling - is present in the book scene but the film corpus does not show the direct confrontation scene (Harry stepping out from behind the bookshelves), only the overheard conversation. The prefect-status conflict over the DA list is absent from the film corpus.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The core motivation (fear, suspicion, self-importance) is present. The internal conflict dimension - particularly the DA signing hesitation that shows Ernie torn between conviction and self-preservation - is absent from the film corpus.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: Motivations are simplified but not wrong. The film shows why Ernie suspects Harry. What is lost is the more nuanced conflict between his stated values and his self-interest (prefect status). This is a simplification rather than a distortion.
motivations_internal_conflict.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for CoS motivation. Not enough evidence for later motivational complexity.

character_arc.book_baseline: Ernie has a clear minor arc across CoS: he begins as the most vocal accuser of Harry, sustains that position stubbornly even when confronted, and then - once the real culprit is exposed - formally and publicly apologizes, extending his hand. This arc (accusation to apology) is complete within CoS. Beyond CoS, he develops from suspicious outsider to DA member and ally, culminating in active combat loyalty in OotP and DH. The broader arc is: suspicious Hufflepuff -> reluctant ally -> committed fighter.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The film corpus shows only the accusation phase of the CoS arc. The apology and reconciliation (book scenes 38-44) are absent from the film corpus. The broader arc toward DA membership and combat loyalty is also absent.
character_arc.difference: The resolution of the CoS arc - the apology, the handshake, the shift to working alongside Harry - is not present in the film corpus. This is a meaningful omission because the apology is the payoff of the CoS subplot and establishes Ernie as someone capable of revising his views with dignity.
character_arc.penalty_logic: The arc is incomplete in the film corpus: the starting point is shown but the turning point and resolution are absent. This compresses the arc to the point where only the negative phase exists in the film. If the apology scene was filmed and simply not included in the corpus provided, the score would be higher - but based on available evidence, the arc is truncated.
character_arc.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for the accusation phase. Not enough evidence in the film corpus for the resolution phase.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Ernie's key relationships are: (1) Hannah Abbott - his constant companion, to whom he plays the role of authority and explainer; (2) Harry - an adversarial-to-ally dynamic that moves from suspicion through formal apology to genuine camaraderie; (3) the DA group - a collective relationship of shared purpose. The Hannah dynamic is one of Ernie performing for an audience; the Harry dynamic is the most dramatically significant.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The film shows Ernie with Hannah in the library scene, preserving the dynamic of Ernie as the authority figure explaining things to her. The Harry relationship is shown only in its hostile phase (overheard conversation) without the confrontation or reconciliation. The DA relationship is absent from the film corpus.
key_relationships.difference: The Hannah dynamic is preserved in tone. The Harry relationship is shown only partially - the suspicion without the resolution removes the full arc of that dynamic. The DA camaraderie is absent.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The Hannah relationship is faithful in the scene shown. The Harry relationship is incomplete - the film corpus shows only the hostile phase, which changes the overall emotional dynamic of that relationship from adversarial-to-ally to simply adversarial.
key_relationships.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for Hannah dynamic. Reasonably inferred that Harry relationship is incomplete based on absence of reconciliation in film corpus.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Ernie's complexity comes from the tension between his pomposity and his genuine moral seriousness; between his self-importance and his real courage (publicly endorsing Harry, casting a Patronus, demanding the right to fight); between his stubborn wrongness in CoS and his dignified self-correction; between his prefect pride and his willingness to risk it for the DA. He is also comic (OWL revision boasting, pirouetting Apparition attempt) without being merely comic. These contradictions make him more than a one-note pompous Hufflepuff.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The film corpus shows only the pompous-suspicious facet. The courage, the self-correction, the prefect anxiety, the DA commitment, the combat loyalty, the comic OWL obsession, the Patronus - none of these appear in the film corpus. The character is reduced to his least flattering moment.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: Significant nuance is absent: the apology (moral seriousness), the DA hesitation (internal conflict), the public endorsement of Harry (courage), the Patronus (active heroism), the Great Hall stand (defiance). What remains is recognizable but one-dimensional.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: The film corpus presents only the suspicious-pompous Ernie without the counterweights that make him complex. This is a meaningful reduction. The score reflects that the available film material is faithful as far as it goes, but the absent material substantially changes the audience's understanding of who Ernie is.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for what is present. Reasonably inferred that absent material represents lost complexity based on book corpus.

lost_or_transferred_material: Ernie's formal apology to Harry and handshake in Herbology (CoS book scenes 38-39) - absent from film corpus
Ernie's public declaration of support for Harry at the Hog's Head ('I personally believe you one hundred per cent') - absent from film corpus
Ernie's hesitation to sign the DA list due to prefect-status anxiety, then overcoming it - absent from film corpus
Ernie's OWL revision obsession and boasting (eight or nine hours a day) - absent from film corpus
Ernie casting a Patronus (boar) to save Harry, Ron, and Hermione from dementors in DH - absent from film corpus
Ernie standing up in the Great Hall to demand the right to stay and fight in DH - absent from film corpus
Ernie's satisfaction at Malfoy's comeuppance on the Hogwarts Express (OotP) - absent from film corpus
Ernie confronted directly by Harry in the library (the trembling-but-stubborn exchange) - absent from film corpus, replaced by Harry only overhearing and slipping away
score_caps_applied: Cap 3 (score above 75 if core book agency transferred or removed): Ernie's Patronus scene and Great Hall defiance are absent from the film corpus, representing lost agency. Score held at 75.
comparative.key_observations: The film faithfully reproduces Ernie's most memorable book scene - the pompous, conspiratorial CoS library accusation - with high fidelity in voice, tone, and content. However, the film corpus contains almost nothing beyond this single scene, meaning the character is frozen at his least flattering moment. The book Ernie is defined as much by his formal apology, his public courage in OotP, his DA commitment, and his Patronus in DH as by his early suspicion of Harry. Without those counterweights, the film version is recognizable but one-dimensional. The score of 75 reflects a portrayal that is faithful in what it shows but significantly incomplete in what it omits, with the omissions removing the complexity, arc resolution, and active agency that define the character in the books.
